en.access.editor: Editor rights grant the user permissions to see the DMP and make changes to it. There are no restrictions on the kind of changes this user can make, bar deleting the DMP. The user can not however, assign viewing or editing permissions.
en.access.info: Please note that simultaneous editing of a DMP can lead to data loss as changes may overwrite each other. Please make sure that you are the only one editing the dmp at the moment.
en.access.list: Only contributors in your universities database can be assigned editing rights for a DMP.
en.access.noOwner: Only owners of the DMP are allowed to change access rights.
en.access.no_rights: Default setting. The user will not be able to see or interact with the DMP.
en.access.owner: Owners can view, write, delete and assign permissions for DMPs. They are also capable of creating new owners - handle with care, new owners can remove old ones.
en.admin.banner-missing-info: There is no banner currently set for the app.
en.admin.dialog.banner.captions.title: Title
en.admin.dialog.banner.tooltip.color: Click to select a background color for the banner
en.admin.table.header.title: Title
en.admin.title: Admin page
en.admin.translation-info: In order to see the translations for a specific internal storage, please select the internal storage from the list in the actions menu, and the option 'Translations'.
en.ui.consent.agreement: I hereby declare that I have been informed about the information obligations (right to information / correction / deletion, etc.) according to article 12 - 21 DSGVO and have taken note of them.
en.ui.button.preview.tooltipDisabled: Please save the DMP before being able to preview it.
en.ui.dashboard.dmp.heading: What is a DMP?
en.ui.dashboard.dmp.text.middle: is a structured document that keeps record of what research data is created and what happens to that data during and after a project. It helps with planning the research process, managing your data in accordance with the
en.ui.dashboard.dmp.text.suffix: , and defining rights and responsibilities in a research project involving several researchers or institutions.
en.ui.dashboard.instructions.create.descrip: Start creating a new DMP by following guided steps. Save your progress, invite colleagues, and export the DMP as a document for submission.
en.ui.dashboard.instructions.create.heading: CREATE NEW DMP
en.ui.dashboard.instructions.description: Learn how to create a DMP step-by-step, including exporting drafts, editing, and updating your DMP according to funder requirements.
en.ui.dashboard.instructions.export.body: Depending on the funder of your project, the exported DMP will either reflect the funder’s DMP template (FWF, Horizon Europe) or the DMP recommendations by Science Europe. This happens automatically for imported projects.
en.ui.dashboard.instructions.export.heading: Export the DMP draft as a document.
en.ui.dashboard.instructions.finalise.body: The exported DMP is an automatically created draft that you still need to add the finishing touches to. If you have a choice, we recommend editing in Word. Check the content carefully and complete any missing information, including the DMP version details on the cover sheet. Once ready, you can save your DMP as a PDF and provide it to the funder. Please note that you do not need to transfer your changes back into the DMP tool, as the created DMP document will be the basis for subsequent versions, not the tool.
en.ui.dashboard.instructions.finalise.headi: Finalise your DMP in a text editor.
en.ui.dashboard.instructions.heading: HOW TO CREATE A DMP
en.ui.dashboard.instructions.steps.body: You do not have to do all steps in one go, simply save the status and continue later. You can always go back and jump between steps. You may even invite colleagues from your institution to add information. If you wish, you can version your DMP.
en.ui.dashboard.instructions.steps.heading: Follow the tool through steps 1-10 to provide the required information.
en.ui.dashboard.instructions.update.body: A DMP is a living document and it is recommended to keep it up to date – for yourself, the project team, and the funder. The funders usually ask for updates at certain milestones and at the end of the project. Any changes can easily be done within the document, you do not need the tool for updates. Nevertheless, if you wish to add or change details and create a new version in the DMP tool, for example to update the machine-actionable information, remember to carefully check and complete the exported DMP as described above.
en.ui.dashboard.instructions.update.heading: Regularly update your DMP.
Pre-filling content with detailed information from your CRIS application and other systems
Suggesting answers that you can either comply with or adjust to your needs
Providing wizards, guidance, and item lists to choose from
en.ui.dashboard.tool.bulletpoints.dmp.text: Guides you step by step through the different sections of a DMP following the
en.ui.dashboard.tool.bulletpoints.document: Exports a pre-filled DMP as a document that you can customize and use for submission to European and national funders
en.ui.dashboard.tool.bulletpoints.madmp.rda: RDA recommendation on machine actionable DMPs
en.ui.dashboard.tool.bulletpoints.supports: Delivers DMPs for FWF and Horizon Europe projects in the funder-specific formats
en.ui.dashboard.tool.description: DAMAP guides you through creating a DMP using pre-filled information and customizable templates. It aligns with Science Europe guidelines and national funder requirements, saving you time and effort.
en.ui.dashboard.tool.heading: About DAMAP
en.ui.dashboard.welcome.message: Welcome to DAMAP, a service that helps you to create the Data Management Plan (DMP) for your project.

en.dmp.steps.costs.infoBox.description: Researchers are encouraged to consider costs arising from data management activities. Data management costs are eligible for many funders and are thus relevant for the proposals. Please follow this

dmp.steps.costs.infoBox.links.title: Data Management Costs Link
dmp.steps.costs.infoBox.links.text: to find out more about possible data management costs that may also be eligible for funding.

en.dmp.steps.costs.instructions: In this step, you will provide information on the resources required to manage your data and a clear statement on how these costs will be covered.
en.dmp.steps.costs.intro: In this step you are asked to provide a realistic estimation of necessary resources for data management and a clear statement on how these costs will be covered.
en.dmp.steps.costs.label: Costs
en.dmp.steps.costs.question.exist.label: Select the statement which best describes the situation in your project with respect to costs of data management.
en.dmp.steps.costs.question.exist.no: There are no costs dedicated to data management.
en.dmp.steps.costs.question.exist.yes: As outlined below, there are costs dedicated to data management.
en.dmp.steps.costs.question.title: Name
en.dmp.steps.costs.summaryLine: Estimate the costs for data management in this project.

en.dmp.steps.data.deletion.question.label: Does this dataset have to be deleted, e.g. for contractual, regulatory, or ethical reasons? If yes, please add an estimated date for the deletion, the reason for deletion, and a responsible person.
en.dmp.steps.data.reuse.instructions: This step helps you think about future research uses or users of your data and what potential users may require to access your data.
en.dmp.steps.data.reuse.intro: This step helps you to think about foreseeable research uses or users of your data sets and what potential reusers require to access your data.

en.dmp.steps.data.specify.instructions: In this step, you specify the datasets (i.e., groups of data with similar properties) you will produce or reuse in your project.
en.dmp.steps.data.specify.intro.default: Research data comes in all forms and sizes. The table below helps you to describe the data sets (i.e. groups of data with similar properties) you are expecting to create during the project.
en.dmp.steps.data.specify.intro.reused: Research data comes in all forms and sizes. The table below helps you to describe the data sets (i.e. groups of data with similar properties) you are expecting to reuse during the project.
en.dmp.steps.data.specify.label: Specify research data
en.dmp.steps.data.specify.question.kind: What kinds of research data will you create?
en.dmp.steps.data.specify.question.nodata: Please explain why you won't create or analyse any research data.
en.dmp.steps.data.specify.question.reused: What kinds of research data will you reuse?
en.dmp.steps.data.specify.tab.introStorage: This step helps you to document who is allowed to see or edit your data in the project phase and to find good storage and backup solutions for your data.
en.dmp.steps.documentation.instructions: This step helps you to provide information on how data will be organised during the project, which data quality control measures will be used, what documentation will be prepared and made available to enable the reuse of data, and which metadata will be provided.
en.dmp.steps.documentation.intro: This step helps you to provide information on how data will be organised during the project, which data quality control measures are used, what documentation will be prepared and made available to enable reuse of data, and which metadata will be provided.
en.dmp.steps.documentation.label: Documentation and data quality
en.dmp.steps.documentation.question.dataQua: How will the consistency and quality of data collection be controlled?
en.dmp.steps.documentation.question.datagen: How will the research data be generated or reused and which methods and software will be used?
en.dmp.steps.documentation.question.documen: How will you provide documentation needed to validate data analysis and facilitate data reuse?
en.dmp.steps.documentation.question.metadat: Please indicate which metadata will be provided to help others identify, discover and reuse the data.
en.dmp.steps.documentation.question.otherDa: Please specify what other measures are taken:
en.dmp.steps.documentation.question.structu: How will you structure the data and handle versioning?
en.dmp.steps.documentation.summaryLine: Outline how your data will be organised throughout the project.

en.dmp.steps.end.instructions: Below is a summary of the information you provided. To add more information, go to the previous step(s).

en.dmp.steps.legal.instructions: This step helps you consider potential issues concerning personal data, intellectual property rights, and ownership and asks you for basic information on the handling of ethical issues within this project.
en.dmp.steps.legal.intro: This step helps you consider potential issues concerning personal data, intellectual property rights and ownership and asks you for basic information on the handling of ethical issues within this project.

en.dmp.steps.licensing.hint: Please indicate which datasets you plan to keep closed or publish. Select a license for the datasets you want to publish and estimate the release date.
en.dmp.steps.licensing.info: Choose a license from the list or use the license selector to find an appropriate license.
en.dmp.steps.licensing.instructions: This step helps you to specify the access to your research output in the future: Which of your datasets will be published, which will be provided to interested persons on request only, and which need to be under lock and key, for contractual, regulatory, or ethical reasons? It also reminds you to choose a license for your open data that clearly describes the reuse conditions for potential users. To comply with the FAIR principles, metadata should be published, even if the data is closed or restricted.
en.dmp.steps.licensing.intro: This step helps you to specify the access to your research output in the future: Which of your data sets will be published, which will be provided to interested persons on request only and which need to be under lock and key, for contractual, regulatory, or ethical reasons? It also reminds you to choose a license for your open data that clearly describes the reuse conditions for potential reusers. To comply with the FAIR principles, metadata should be published, even if the data are closed or restricted.
en.dmp.steps.licensing.label: Data access and licensing
en.dmp.steps.licensing.question.closedAcces: Explain why the data will not be accessible (closed access):

en.dmp.steps.licensing.warning.personalData: You indicated in step 6 that this dataset contains sensitive personal data. Do you really want to publish the dataset open, without restrictions?

en.dmp.steps.people.contact.title: Project contact
en.dmp.steps.people.instructions: In this step, you add individuals to the project and provide additional information about them, for example, their role in the project. To mark someone as the contact person click the person-with-envelope icon next to their role. You can change the existing information and add more contributors via the search (choose a search service and start typing the name). Please provide a minimum of one contact person as well as a data manager (it can be the same person).
en.dmp.steps.people.intro: In this step, you add people involved in the project and provide additional info about them, e.g., their role. In this example, the project leader’s info has already been added and marked as the contact person (note icon with person and envelope next to their name). You can add more contributors via the search field (ensure you select the appropriate database, e.g., ORCID), or manually via the “Add contributor manually button”. Please provide a minimum of one contact person as well as a data manager (it can be the same person).

en.dmp.steps.repositories.heading: Repositories
en.dmp.steps.repositories.instructions: Repositories are used for sustainable data management beyond the lifetime of the project and to make data discoverable and reusable to others. If available, use a domain specific repository for your data. The search filter below may help you to find one.
en.dmp.steps.repositories.intro: Use repositories to sustainably manage your data after the project’s life and to make your data discoverable and reusable. If possible, use a domain specific repository for your data. The search filter below can help you to find one. You’ll also want to set the time for how long your data should remain in the repository. This retention period can be selected in the Set retention period for datasets section

en.ui.consent.duration: Personal data that is collected for registration is generally only stored for the duration of the service made available, unless a longer retention period is mandated by statutory regulations.
en.dmp.enum.compliance.ANONYMISATION: by anonymisation of personal data for preservation and/or sharing (truly anonymous data are no longer considered personal data)
en.dmp.enum.compliance.ENCRYPTION: by encryption of personal data (the encryption key must be stored separately from the data, for instance by a trusted third party)
en.dmp.enum.compliance.INFORMED_CONSENT: by gaining informed consent for processing personal data
en.dmp.enum.compliance.PSEUDONYMISATION: by pseudonymisation of personal data (the main difference with anonymisation is that pseudonymisation is reversible)
en.dmp.enum.securitymeasure.LOCKED_STORAGE: keep printouts of sensitive data in locked storage

en.info.cris: Option currently selected in the CRIS system: {{value}}.
en.info.dataset.none:
Research data is all digital objects created in the course of academic work, for instance, measurements, surveys, observations, simulations, interviews, and software.
If you still believe you will not generate or reuse any data in your project, we recommend manually filling in the original DMP templates instead of using the DMP tool.
FWF: Please fill in the required administrative information in section I.1 and explain why the project will not produce or use data in section I.2 Data management responsibilities and resources. The rest of the template should be empty.
EU (Horizon Europe): Please fill in the required administrative information on pages 1-3 and explain why the project will not produce or use data in section 1 Data summary. If applicable, enter information in section 3 Other research outputs.
en.info.dataset.unknown: This is a good time to think about your expectations. You should already have an idea of what kind of (digital) material you will be creating or analysing in your project, even if it is still vague and you don’t have specific details. If this is the case, click on “Specify datasets.” and describe the expected material as well as possible in this early stage. You can mention in the DMP (= your export document from the tool) that the data description is a broad estimation that will be updated in a later version of the DMP. If you come to the conclusion that you will not create or analyse any data, click on “No data will be created or analysed.” and follow the instructions there.
en.info.documentation.metadata:
In archives, metadata is used to create consistent and structured descriptions of resources and their characteristics. Metadata is independent and can either be stored together with the resources it describes or separately. Metadata plays a key role in the future of your stored research data: it contains important information which for example enables you and others to search for and find your data.
A metadata set consists mainly of descriptive categories, the so-called elements and their values. To facilitate communication with other systems, predefined standards are used for the elements and controlled vocabularies for the values.
en.info.ethical.committeeReviewed.prefix:
Please note that you must address (potential) ethical issues in separate documents according to the requirements of the relevant funding body. The DMP only serves to help identify some ethical issues.
Please contact your
en.info.ethical.committeeReviewed.suffix: for additional information and support. You must do so if your planned research involves any human participation.
en.info.ethical.ethicalIssues.text: Your research plan may require ethical review. Inform yourself by consulting your
en.info.ethical.humanParticipants.prefix: An informed consent for participation (in addition to an informed consent to data processing) is required. For more information, see an
en.info.project.funder: If your project is missing, please contact [mail@address.example]. You can add project information manually, however you will have to select a funder template when exporting as funder information is not available.
en.info.repositories.retention: Set the time for how long your data should remain in the repository

en.dmp.license.wizard.legalWarning: This is not legal advice. Please choose your license carefully.
en.dmp.license.wizard.license.agpl1.descrip: The GNU Affero General Public License is a free, copyleft license for software and other kinds of works, specifically designed to ensure cooperation with the community in the case of network server software.
en.dmp.license.wizard.license.agpl1.name: Affero General Public License v1.0 only
en.dmp.license.wizard.license.agpl1plus.des: The GNU Affero General Public License is a free, copyleft license for software and other kinds of works, specifically designed to ensure cooperation with the community in the case of network server software. Allows content under this "any later version" grant to be redistributed under the GPL-3.0-or-later.
en.dmp.license.wizard.license.agpl1plus.nam: Affero General Public License v1.0 or later
en.dmp.license.wizard.license.agpl3.descrip: The AGPL license differs from the other GNU licenses in that it was built for network software. You can distribute modified versions if you keep track of the changes and the date you made them. As per usual with GNU licenses, you must license derivatives under AGPL. It provides the same restrictions and freedoms as the GPLv3 but with an additional clause which makes it so that source code must be distributed along with web publication. Since web sites and services are never distributed in the traditional sense, the AGPL is the GPL of the web.
en.dmp.license.wizard.license.agpl3.name: GNU Affero General Public License v3.0 or later
en.dmp.license.wizard.license.agpl3plus.des: The AGPL license differs from the other GNU licenses in that it was built for network software. You can distribute modified versions if you keep track of the changes and the date you made them. As per usual with GNU licenses, you must license derivatives under AGPL. It provides the same restrictions and freedoms as the GPLv3 but with an additional clause which makes it so that source code must be distributed along with web publication.
